content:
Boqun Feng said:

But unlocked spinlock is safe to move

hmm at the moment pinning in rust is a one-time thing, so unpinning and later repinning does not exist. If we really need this then we might be able to implement it via dropping the old value and immediately initializing an unpinned value with the same fields in the same location. Or try to get it implemented on the rust side. I could imagine a trait that would make it possible:

pub unsafe trait Unpinnable {
    fn try_unpin<P: DerefMut>(self: Pin<P>) -> Result<P, Pin<P>>;
}

But are there reasons to move a spinlock after locking it?

y86 said:

Boqun Feng said:

But unlocked spinlock is safe to move

hmm at the moment pinning in rust is a one-time thing, so unpinning and later repinning does not exist. If we really need this then we might be able to implement it via dropping the old value and immediately initializing an unpinned value with the same fields in the same location. Or try to get it implemented on the rust side. I could imagine a trait that would make it possible:

pub unsafe trait Unpinnable {
    fn try_unpin<P: DerefMut>(self: Pin<P>) -> Result<P, Pin<P>>;
}

But are there reasons to move a spinlock after locking it?

One situation I have in my mind about this Unpinnable is:

let some_work = Work::new(...); // create a work on stack, because it's fast. Assuming Work: !Unpin
let pinned = unsafe { Pin::new_unchecked(&some_work) };
// publish the work
schedule_work(pinned.as_ref());
if wait_for_work_done(pinned.as_ref(), timeout) { // sleep wait, return true if timeout.
  if cancel_work(pinned) { // return true if cancel succeeds, i.e. work hasn't executed.
    // slow path, we have to execute the work after the function returns.
    // Now `some_work` is unpublished, so in theory it's safe to move.
    let boxed_work = Box::new(some_work); // move to the heap
                                          // why not recreate the work? Because Work::new(..) has non-trivial computation.
    schedule_work(boxed_work.into_pin());
  }
}

I know this is totally UB, and probably a footgun, but I'm not suprised if one wants this works. And I don't find a (simple) sound way to make it work yet.

One safe way to make it work is providing:

impl Work {
    pub fn content(self: Pin<&mut self>) -> Pin<&mut Content> {
        // `Content` is the `Unpin` part in the work
    }
}
But it has some layout requirement for Work.

Or use ptr::read?

let some_work = Work::new(...); // create a work on stack, because it's fast. Assuming Work: !Unpin
let pinned = unsafe { Pin::new_unchecked(&some_work) };
// publish the work
schedule_work(pinned.as_ref());
if wait_for_work_done(pinned.as_ref(), timeout) { // sleep wait, return true if timeout.
  if cancel_work(pinned.as_ref()) { // return true if cancel succeeds, i.e. work hasn't executed.
    // slow path, we have to execute the work after the function returns.
    // Now `some_work` is unpublished, so in theory it's safe to move.
    let boxed_work = Box::new(unsafe { ptr::read(pinned.get_ref() as *const _) }); // copy instead of moving
    schedule_work(boxed_work.into_pin());
  }
}

hmm both still are technically UB, since you are moving a pinned object. Is there some way to create a function that takes a pinned work and essentially just copies it to a different location?

impl Work {
    pub fn copy_from(work: &Work) -> Box<Self> { ... }
}
I thought ptr::read means copying, no?
well yes, it is essentially "moving but without destroying the source"

But the doc says:

Reads the value from src without moving it. This leaves the memory in src unchanged.
but if we ptr::read a linked list and then deallocate the original memory, the linked list will break

y86 said:

but if we ptr::read a linked list and then deallocate the original memory, the linked list will break

True, but the new linked list is not in pinned state, so it's fine?
superficially it is, but I am not sure what the rules are in that scenario...
You may be right, since we lose the protection provided by pin-init
what do you mean?
I mean with ptr::read, you can get a object which is !Unpin, but it has not been pinned initialised.
Of course, if we say ptr::read on the a pinned object is not UB.

let a = pinned_inited(...);

// SAFETY: a is a valid pointer
let b = unsafe { ptr::read(a.get_ref() as *const _) };

// SAFETY: no one will move b.
let p = unsafe { Pin::new_unchecked(&b) }; // but `b` has a incorrect linked list in it.
Oh now I get what you meant by losing the protection. Yes that can happen, but that is another safety requirement on ptr::read, see here.
well it is not really a safety requirement, but a heads-up
I am not sure why this is not listed at the top
but after ptr::read, I indeed don't use a or the memory pointed by a.
well, it still gets dropped
I think it has to be UB, it is moving the pinned value
Right, but if dropping is an issue, then you cannot use ptr::read on any !Copy, right?
normally you would mem::forget the old value
or overwrite it via ptr::write
ah, right, I mis-read the link you pasted.

y86 said:

or overwrite it via ptr::write

And ptr::write is considered as move. Ok.
in my mind, a move is a ptr::drop_in_place and then a ptr::write

doc says:

Additionally, it does not drop src. Semantically, src is moved into the location pointed to by dst.

so x = y; is

ptr::drop_in_place(&raw mut x);
ptr::write(&raw mut x, y);
ah right it takes y by value
in a normal move though the destination is first dropped
Now, I guess it's fair to say ptr::read doesn't work for this.

y86 said:

hmm both still are technically UB, since you are moving a pinned object. Is there some way to create a function that takes a pinned work and essentially just copies it to a different location?

impl Work {
    pub fn copy_from(work: &Work) -> Box<Self> { ... }
}

It's possible, just not a generic solution.
Why can't this be generalized?
The function means do a bit-wise copy of Work, and pinned initialisation before return, right?
If so, how can we do a generic bit-wise copy of any type?

Boqun Feng said:

The function means do a bit-wise copy of Work, and pinned initialisation before return, right?

kinda, it should copy the fields that it is allowed to, so e.g. a list_head would not be allowed.

Boqun Feng said:

If so, how can we do a generic bit-wise copy of any type?

maybe a macro? I first thought of making it a trait. I dont think that we need a general solution, since this problem should affect only a small number of datatypes, right?

y86 said:

Boqun Feng said:

If so, how can we do a generic bit-wise copy of any type?

maybe a macro? I first thought of making it a trait. I dont think that we need a general solution, since this problem should affect only a small number of datatypes, right?

Right, and the problem is just a imaginational case.

y86 said:

normally you would mem::forget the old value

Hmm, but the following is still a problem, if ptr::read + forget is not UB or considered moving on pinned objects:

let data = ..;
let a = pinned_inited(&mut data); // -> return Pin<>

// SAFETY: a is a valid pointer
let b = unsafe { ptr::read(a.get_ref() as *const _) };

// SAFETY: no one will move b.
let p = unsafe { Pin::new_unchecked(&b) }; // but `b` has a incorrect linked list in it.

forget(data);

this is a move of pinned data IMO

let b = unsafe { ptr::read(a.get_ref() as *const _) };
Then ptr::read doc needs some help?
The ptr::read method duplicates the value. If you then proceed to forget the old one, then you effectively have a move.
I don't think there's anything wrong with saying that its not a move on its own — it's not because the previous location still holds one of the duplicates.
Agreed, but I feel like if you use ptr::read, you end up moving either way (forget or drop/write)
Well, sure.
/me just hope to use ptr::read as bit-wise copy ;-)

ptr::read is a typed copy, so it only copies things relevant to the type in question
for example, it doesn't preserve data in struct padding
Ah, good to know. What I meant is ptr::read doesn't provide the duplication as I thought unless T: Copy.

What do you mean? It can totally be used to duplicate non-Copy types. For example, if you have a struct with integers that doesn't derive Copy, then duplicating it with ptr::read is perfectly fine in the language's eyes.

I think that there are two different notions of "move" that are used in the docs/here, I will call them:

  • "rust" move: "x = y;" a move, i.e. dropping x, copying y into x and then dropping y
  • semantic move: if we have some pinned data x and then use ptr::read to copy it into some other location calling it y and then call some function on y. Then this object can observe the change in its memory location, i.e. it has moved.

When talking about "pinned data is not allowed to be moved" I think of the semantic kind.
In that sense, ptr::read on Copy is also a semantic move?
yes

y86 said:

yes

Then is "normal copy" a semantic move?
with normal copy, do you mean x = y where x,y: Copy? and do you mean "rust" move?

y86 said:

with normal copy, do you mean x = y where x,y: Copy? and do you mean "rust" move?

Yes on "what normal copy means", and by "move" I mean semantics move.
yes, x = y is a rust move and that always is a semantic move.

Usually the word "move" carries the connotation that the original is now invalid, which isn't the case for copy types. However, if you perform a copy and pretend that the original has become invalid and don't touch it again, then it is exactly like a move. (Since, for copy types, making the original valid is a no-op, since copy types don't have destructors.)
So for copy types, a move is a copy followed by a no-op.

let x = ..; // data is Copy + Clone but !Unpin

let pin = unsafe { Pin::new_unchecked(&x) };

let y = x; // UB

let y = x.clone(); // not UB?

Boqun Feng said:

let x = ..; // data is Copy + Clone but !Unpin

let pin = unsafe { Pin::new_unchecked(&x) };

let y = x; // UB

let y = x.clone(); // not UB?

Indeed, clone allows me to do something more.

Pinning is a safety invariant and not a validity invariant, so breaking its promises does not, strictly speaking, lead to language UB. You only get true UB if you write unsafe code that relies on the pinning guarantees, and then break the pinning guarantees in a way that the unsafe code did not expect.
https://www.ralfj.de/blog/2018/08/22/two-kinds-of-invariants.html

Interesting, so can I say the following is not UB?

let some_work = Work::new(...); // create a work on stack, because it's fast. Assuming Work: !Unpin
let pinned = unsafe { Pin::new_unchecked(&some_work) };
// publish the work
schedule_work(pinned.as_ref());
if wait_for_work_done(pinned.as_ref(), timeout) { // sleep wait, return true if timeout.
  if cancel_work(pinned) { // return true if cancel succeeds, i.e. work hasn't executed.
    // slow path, we have to execute the work after the function returns.
    // Now `some_work` is unpublished, so in theory it's safe to move.

    // I don't need pinning safety guarantee here.
    let boxed_work = Box::new(some_work); // move to the heap
                                          // why not recreate the work? Because Work::new(..) has non-trivial computation.
    schedule_work(boxed_work.into_pin());
  }
}
"Yes, I moved after pinned, but no I don't need it to be pinned"
A type can definitely promise you that it has become safe to move again after having been pinned.
do you have any documentation link to that? like a UCG issue or something else?
It follows from pinning being a safety invariant, and I suspect I could find something on that if I tried.

y86 said:

do you have any documentation link to that? like a UCG issue or something else?

FWIW, pin doc does say:

It is sometimes useful to have objects that are guaranteed not to move, in the sense that their placement in memory does not change, and can thus be relied upon. A prime example of such a scenario would be building self-referential structs, as moving an object with pointers to itself will invalidate them, which could cause undefined behavior.
not "which is an undefined behavior"

Alice Ryhl said:

A type can definitely promise you that it has become safe to move again after having been pinned.

yay

Boqun Feng said:

y86 said:

do you have any documentation link to that? like a UCG issue or something else?

FWIW, pin doc does say:

It is sometimes useful to have objects that are guaranteed not to move, in the sense that their placement in memory does not change, and can thus be relied upon. A prime example of such a scenario would be building self-referential structs, as moving an object with pointers to itself will invalidate them, which could cause undefined behavior.

I meant reffering to this:
Alice Ryhl said:

A type can definitely promise you that it has become safe to move again after having been pinned.

i.e. having types that allow being unpinned without being dropped
I can't really find any discussion about pinning in the UCG repository at all?
I guess this one fits? https://github.com/rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines/issues/148
I guess.

Using terminology from Two Kinds of Invariants: Safety and Validity 3, basically every aspect of Pin is in the realm of "safety invariants", not "validity invariants". This means that violating these rules might not immediately cause UB in a way that Miri can detect or that could go wrong at runtime, it "just" means that invariants are broken and if control enters any code that depends on these invariants, it's your fault when that code goes wrong.
https://internals.rust-lang.org/t/pin-meaning-of-invalidation/17693/3

y86 said:

Boqun Feng said:

y86 said:

do you have any documentation link to that? like a UCG issue or something else?

FWIW, pin doc does say:

It is sometimes useful to have objects that are guaranteed not to move, in the sense that their placement in memory does not change, and can thus be relied upon. A prime example of such a scenario would be building self-referential structs, as moving an object with pointers to itself will invalidate them, which could cause undefined behavior.

I meant reffering to this:
Alice Ryhl said:

A type can definitely promise you that it has become safe to move again after having been pinned.

i.e. having types that allow being unpinned without being dropped

My point is, if moving after !Unpin pinned is not a 100% UB (or 100% cause UB), then it means it's allowed.

Put it in another way, IIUC, moving !Unpin after pinned is unsound (not safe), but not always a UB (not valid). It's something allowed since: You must always be valid, but you only must be safe in safe code.
But of course, it's up to the operational semantics to say the above code doesn't have UB.

Yesterday I had totally forgotten about the Safety vs Validity invariants. As Alice already pointed out, this is a safety invariant, and thus we could define our own invariant for some type (that is !Unpin) allowing conditional unpinning. While I think we still should ask t-opsem for any additional info about this, but ultimately I think the possibility of creating such a type lies with us.
(I do not think that this would be a thing big enough for the std-lib ATM)

I don't think there's anything special about opsem here. Pin is a purely library thing. Unpin does have opsem implications -- so you shouldn't accidentally make your !Unpin type Unpin -- but it's fine for an unpinned type to be !Unpin. You probably will miss some optimisation there, but it won't cause additional UBs.
I'm lost on "but it's fine for an unpinned type to be !Unpin", could you elaborate?
You can make your type !Unpin even though you never pin it and move it freely.
